questions.label: What is real?
questions.framing: Metaphysics — the nature of being itself.

questions.label: What can I know?
questions.framing: Epistemology — knowledge, its sources and limits.

questions.label: What is the self?
questions.framing: Mind & identity — what, if anything, the 'I' is.

questions.label: How should I live?
questions.framing: Ethics — the good life and right action.

questions.label: What is a just society?
questions.framing: Politics — power, justice, the legitimate order.

questions.label: Is the will free?
questions.framing: Freedom & determinism — whether we author our acts.

questions.label: Why do we suffer?
questions.framing: Liberation — the root of suffering and the way to peace.

questions.label: How do we transform?
questions.framing: Transformation — how minds change, heal, and awaken.

questions.label: Is there an Absolute?
questions.framing: The divine, the ground of being, ultimate reality.

concepts.gloss: True reality is eternal, perfect Forms; the visible world is their shadow.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Republic (Books VI–VII)
readings.note: The Sun, Line and Cave — the Forms unveiled.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Phaedo
readings.note: The soul's recollection of the Forms.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Parmenides
readings.note: Plato's own probing critique of the theory.

concepts.gloss: Every substance is matter shaped by form; reality is here, not in a Form-heaven.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Metaphysics (Books VII–IX)
readings.note: Substance as matter informed by form.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Physics (Book II)
readings.note: The doctrine of the four causes.

concepts.gloss: Everything is atoms moving in the void; no purpose, only matter and space.

readings.author: Lucretius
readings.title: On the Nature of Things
readings.note: The fullest surviving atomist work, in verse.

readings.author: Epicurus
readings.title: Letter to Herodotus
readings.note: Atoms and void, stated briefly.

concepts.gloss: Change is illusion; what truly is, is single, eternal and unchanging.

readings.author: Parmenides
readings.title: On Nature (fragments)
readings.note: The 'Way of Truth': what is, is one.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Parmenides
readings.note: A dramatized stress-test of Eleatic monism.

concepts.gloss: All is becoming; you cannot step in the same river twice.

readings.author: Heraclitus
readings.title: Fragments
readings.note: The river, the fire, the logos.

readings.author: Charles Kahn
readings.title: The Art and Thought of Heraclitus
readings.note: The standard scholarly reconstruction.

concepts.gloss: Only physical matter exists; mind and spirit are matter in motion.

readings.author: Baron d'Holbach
readings.title: The System of Nature
readings.note: A thoroughgoing materialist universe.

readings.author: Julien La Mettrie
readings.title: Man a Machine
readings.note: The body — and mind — as mechanism.

concepts.gloss: To be is to be perceived; reality is fundamentally mental, not material.

readings.author: George Berkeley
readings.title: A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge
readings.note: 'To be is to be perceived.'

readings.author: George Berkeley
readings.title: Three Dialogues between Hylas and Philonous
readings.note: The same case, in lively dialogue.

concepts.gloss: Reality is Spirit (Geist) coming to know itself through history.

readings.author: G.W.F. Hegel
readings.title: Phenomenology of Spirit
readings.note: Spirit's odyssey toward self-knowledge.

readings.author: Charles Taylor
readings.title: Hegel
readings.note: The most navigable guide to a hard thinker.

concepts.gloss: God and Nature are one infinite substance; everything is a mode of it.

readings.author: Baruch Spinoza
readings.title: Ethics
readings.note: One substance — God-or-Nature — proved geometrically.

concepts.gloss: The basic units of reality are events and becomings, not static things.

readings.author: Alfred North Whitehead
readings.title: Process and Reality
readings.note: Reality as events and becomings.

readings.author: Henri Bergson
readings.title: Creative Evolution
readings.note: Duration and the élan vital.

concepts.gloss: Nothing has inherent, independent existence; all is empty of fixed essence.

readings.author: Nāgārjuna
readings.title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā
readings.note: The root verses on the Middle Way.

readings.author: Jay Garfield (trans.)
readings.title: The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way
readings.note: Translation with lucid commentary.

concepts.gloss: Nothing stands alone; each thing arises in dependence on conditions.

readings.author: (Pali Canon)
readings.title: Saṃyutta Nikāya (Nidāna-saṃyutta)
readings.note: The chain of conditioned arising.

readings.author: Bhikkhu Bodhi
readings.title: In the Buddha's Words
readings.note: A clear thematic anthology of the suttas.

concepts.gloss: A single non-dual absolute underlies all; the many-world is appearance (maya).

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: The Principal Upanishads
readings.note: Chandogya & Brihadaranyaka on the absolute.

readings.author: Ādi Śaṅkara
readings.title: Brahma Sūtra Bhāṣya
readings.note: The classic non-dual reading.

concepts.gloss: An ineffable source and pattern flows through all things; name it and you miss it.

readings.author: Laozi
readings.title: Tao Te Ching
readings.note: The way that can be named is not the Way.

readings.author: Zhuangzi
readings.title: Zhuangzi
readings.note: Playful, radical Daoist parables.

concepts.gloss: Reality is the dynamic interplay of complementary polarities and vital energy.

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: I Ching (Book of Changes)
readings.note: The cosmology of changing polarities.

readings.author: Fung Yu-lan
readings.title: A Short History of Chinese Philosophy
readings.note: Sets yin-yang in its philosophical context.

concepts.gloss: Reality is composed of countless windowless minds (monads) in pre-set harmony.

readings.author: G.W. Leibniz
readings.title: Monadology
readings.note: Reality as windowless minds in harmony.

readings.author: G.W. Leibniz
readings.title: Discourse on Metaphysics
readings.note: The system's earlier, fuller sketch.

concepts.gloss: We mistake shadows for reality; knowledge is the painful turn toward the light.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Republic (Book VII)
readings.note: Shadows, ascent, and the painful turn to light.

concepts.gloss: Reason, not the senses, is the road to certain knowledge.

readings.author: René Descartes
readings.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
readings.note: Doubt rebuilt into certainty by reason.

readings.author: G.W. Leibniz
readings.title: New Essays on Human Understanding
readings.note: The rationalist reply to Locke.

concepts.gloss: All knowledge begins in sense experience; the mind starts blank.

readings.author: David Hume
readings.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
readings.note: The sharpest, most readable statement.

readings.author: John Locke
readings.title: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding
readings.note: The founding empiricist treatise.

concepts.gloss: I can doubt everything but the doubting; 'I think, therefore I am.'

readings.author: René Descartes
readings.title: Meditations on First Philosophy (II)
readings.note: 'I think, therefore I am.'

readings.author: René Descartes
readings.title: Discourse on the Method
readings.note: The first, plainer formulation.

concepts.gloss: The mind at birth is a blank slate written on by experience.

readings.author: John Locke
readings.title: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (Book II)
readings.note: No innate ideas; experience writes the mind.

concepts.gloss: Suspend judgment; certainty is unreachable, and peace lies in not grasping it.

readings.author: Sextus Empiricus
readings.title: Outlines of Pyrrhonism
readings.note: Suspend judgment, and find tranquility.

concepts.gloss: We never know things-in-themselves, only a world shaped by the mind's categories.

readings.author: Immanuel Kant
readings.title: Critique of Pure Reason
readings.note: We know appearances shaped by the mind.

readings.author: Immanuel Kant
readings.title: Prolegomena to Any Future Metaphysics
readings.note: Kant's own shorter way in.

concepts.gloss: A claim is scientific only if some possible observation could refute it.

readings.author: Karl Popper
readings.title: The Logic of Scientific Discovery
readings.note: Science advances by bold, refutable conjectures.

readings.author: Karl Popper
readings.title: Conjectures and Refutations
readings.note: The idea applied across knowledge.

concepts.gloss: Science changes by revolutions, not steady accumulation; frameworks are incommensurable.

readings.author: Thomas Kuhn
readings.title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
readings.note: Science changes by revolution, not accretion.

concepts.gloss: A statement is meaningful only if it can be verified by experience or is logical.

readings.author: A.J. Ayer
readings.title: Language, Truth and Logic
readings.note: The crisp English manifesto of positivism.

concepts.gloss: There is conventional everyday truth and ultimate truth; both are needed.

readings.author: Nāgārjuna
readings.title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (ch. 24)
readings.note: Conventional and ultimate truth distinguished.

readings.author: Chandrakīrti
readings.title: Madhyamakāvatāra
readings.note: The influential Prāsaṅgika elaboration.

concepts.gloss: Knowledge has valid sources: perception, inference, comparison, testimony.

readings.author: Akṣapāda Gautama
readings.title: Nyāya Sūtras
readings.note: The valid means of knowledge enumerated.

readings.author: B.K. Matilal
readings.title: Perception
readings.note: A modern bridge to Indian epistemology.

concepts.gloss: Truth is many-sided; every claim is partial, true from some standpoint.

readings.author: Umāsvāti
readings.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra
readings.note: The many-sidedness of reality, systematized.

readings.author: Padmanabh Jaini
readings.title: The Jaina Path of Purification
readings.note: The standard scholarly overview.

concepts.gloss: The self is a deathless soul, temporarily housed in the body.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Phaedo
readings.note: Four arguments for the soul's deathlessness.

concepts.gloss: Mind and body are two different substances; the thinking self is non-physical.

readings.author: René Descartes
readings.title: Meditations (VI)
readings.note: Mind and body as distinct substances.

readings.author: Gilbert Ryle
readings.title: The Concept of Mind
readings.note: The famous attack: 'the ghost in the machine.'

concepts.gloss: Your deepest Self is identical with the absolute; 'thou art that.'

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: Chandogya Upanishad
readings.note: 'Tat tvam asi' — thou art that.

readings.author: Ādi Śaṅkara
readings.title: Upadeśasāhasrī
readings.note: The non-dual teaching distilled.

concepts.gloss: There is no permanent self — only a changing bundle of processes.

readings.author: (Pali Canon)
readings.title: Anattalakkhaṇa Sutta
readings.note: The discourse on not-self.

readings.author: Walpola Rahula
readings.title: What the Buddha Taught
readings.note: Still the clearest short introduction.

concepts.gloss: The self is just a bundle of perceptions in flux; no enduring 'I' is found.

readings.author: David Hume
readings.title: A Treatise of Human Nature (I.IV.6)
readings.note: The self as a bundle of perceptions.

concepts.gloss: Most of the mind is hidden; we are driven by forces we don't see.

readings.author: Sigmund Freud
readings.title: The Interpretation of Dreams
readings.note: The royal road to the unconscious.

readings.author: Sigmund Freud
readings.title: Introductory Lectures on Psychoanalysis
readings.note: Freud lecturing on his own system.

concepts.gloss: Beneath the personal psyche lies a shared layer of universal archetypes.

readings.author: Carl Jung
readings.title: The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious
readings.note: Archetypes beneath the personal psyche.

readings.author: Carl Jung
readings.title: Man and His Symbols
readings.note: Jung's last, deliberately accessible book.

concepts.gloss: Drop the inner self; study only observable behavior and conditioning.

readings.author: John B. Watson
readings.title: Psychology as the Behaviorist Views It
readings.note: The behaviorist manifesto.

readings.author: B.F. Skinner
readings.title: Beyond Freedom and Dignity
readings.note: Conditioning over inner freedom.

concepts.gloss: Mind is what mind does; mental states are functional roles, not stuff.

readings.author: Hilary Putnam
readings.title: The Nature of Mental States
readings.note: Mind as functional role, not substance.

readings.author: Jaegwon Kim
readings.title: Philosophy of Mind
readings.note: A clear textbook framing of the debate.

concepts.gloss: Existence precedes essence; there is no fixed self — we make ourselves by choosing.

readings.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
readings.title: Being and Nothingness
readings.note: Existence precedes essence; we make ourselves.

readings.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
readings.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
readings.note: The popular lecture version.

concepts.gloss: The self isn't a thing inside us but our embodied, involved being-among-things.

readings.author: Martin Heidegger
readings.title: Being and Time
readings.note: Dasein as involved being-among-things.

readings.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
readings.title: Phenomenology of Perception
readings.note: The lived, embodied perspective.

concepts.gloss: The 'I' is formed from outside — an image and the gaze of the Other.

readings.author: Jacques Lacan
readings.title: Écrits ('The Mirror Stage')
readings.note: The 'I' formed through image and Other.

concepts.gloss: A person is constituted by their roles and relationships, not isolated within.

readings.author: Confucius
readings.title: Analects
readings.note: The self as woven from its relationships.

readings.author: Herbert Fingarette
readings.title: Confucius: The Secular as Sacred
readings.note: A landmark modern rereading.

concepts.gloss: The self is a project; health is becoming all that one can be.

readings.author: Abraham Maslow
readings.title: Motivation and Personality
readings.note: The hierarchy of needs and its summit.

readings.author: Carl Rogers
readings.title: On Becoming a Person
readings.note: Growth from the therapist's chair.

concepts.gloss: The good life is flourishing through the exercise of virtue and reason.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Nicomachean Ethics
readings.note: Flourishing as the activity of virtue.

concepts.gloss: Virtue lies between extremes — courage between cowardice and recklessness.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Nicomachean Ethics (Book II)
readings.note: Virtue as the mean between extremes.

concepts.gloss: Live according to reason and nature; only virtue is truly good.

readings.author: Epictetus
readings.title: Enchiridion (Handbook)
readings.note: The pocket manual of Stoic practice.

readings.author: Marcus Aurelius
readings.title: Meditations
readings.note: A Stoic emperor's private notebook.

concepts.gloss: The good is pleasure rightly understood — tranquility and absence of pain.

readings.author: Epicurus
readings.title: Letter to Menoeceus
readings.note: Pleasure rightly understood as tranquility.

readings.author: Lucretius
readings.title: On the Nature of Things
readings.note: Epicurean ethics and physics in verse.

concepts.gloss: Act only on a rule you could will everyone to follow; treat people as ends.

readings.author: Immanuel Kant
readings.title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
readings.note: Act on maxims you could universalize.

readings.author: Immanuel Kant
readings.title: Critique of Practical Reason
readings.note: The fuller moral architecture.

concepts.gloss: The right act produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number.

readings.author: John Stuart Mill
readings.title: Utilitarianism
readings.note: The refined, humane statement.

readings.author: Jeremy Bentham
readings.title: Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
readings.note: The original felicific calculus.

concepts.gloss: Cultivate humaneness (ren) through ritual propriety (li) and right relationships.

readings.author: Confucius
readings.title: Analects
readings.note: Humaneness cultivated through ritual.

readings.author: Mencius
readings.title: Mencius
readings.note: Human nature as innately good.

concepts.gloss: Act without forcing; align with the natural flow rather than strive against it.

readings.author: Laozi
readings.title: Tao Te Ching
readings.note: Effortless action aligned with the Way.

readings.author: Edward Slingerland
readings.title: Trying Not to Try
readings.note: Wu wei meets modern psychology.

concepts.gloss: Non-violence toward all living things, in deed, word and thought.

readings.author: (Jain canon)
readings.title: Ācārāṅga Sūtra
readings.note: The earliest doctrine of non-harm.

readings.author: Mahatma Gandhi
readings.title: An Autobiography
readings.note: Ahimsa lived as 'experiments with truth.'

concepts.gloss: Inherited morality is a slave's revolt; revalue values and create your own.

readings.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
readings.title: On the Genealogy of Morality
readings.note: Where our values came from — and against whom.

readings.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
readings.title: Beyond Good and Evil
readings.note: The prelude to the revaluation.

concepts.gloss: To live well is to own your freedom and refuse 'bad faith' — fleeing it.

readings.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
readings.title: Being and Nothingness
readings.note: Freedom, 'bad faith,' and owning one's life.

readings.author: Søren Kierkegaard
readings.title: Either/Or
readings.note: The choice that constitutes a self.

concepts.gloss: Morality grows from relationship and care, not abstract rules.

readings.author: Carol Gilligan
readings.title: In a Different Voice
readings.note: Morality grounded in relationship and care.

readings.author: Nel Noddings
readings.title: Caring
readings.note: Care as the basis of ethics and education.

concepts.gloss: The just city is ruled by those who know the good; each part in its place.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Republic
readings.note: Justice in the soul and the ideal city.

concepts.gloss: Legitimate authority rests on an agreement among the governed.

readings.author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
readings.title: The Social Contract
readings.note: 'Man is born free; everywhere in chains.'

readings.author: John Locke
readings.title: Two Treatises of Government
readings.note: Consent, rights, and limited government.

concepts.gloss: Without a strong sovereign, life is a war of all against all.

readings.author: Thomas Hobbes
readings.title: Leviathan
readings.note: Order through an absolute sovereign.

concepts.gloss: All have rights to life, liberty and property that the state must protect.

readings.author: John Locke
readings.title: Two Treatises of Government
readings.note: Life, liberty, and property as rights.

readings.author: Mary Wollstonecraft
readings.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
readings.note: Natural rights extended, against their gendered limits.

concepts.gloss: Legitimacy flows from the people's collective will toward the common good.

readings.author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
readings.title: The Social Contract
readings.note: Legitimacy as the people's collective will.

concepts.gloss: History is driven by class struggle over the means of production.

readings.author: Karl Marx & Friedrich Engels
readings.title: The Communist Manifesto
readings.note: History as the history of class struggle.

readings.author: Karl Marx
readings.title: Capital, Volume I
readings.note: The deep critique of capital.

concepts.gloss: Choose principles of justice from behind a 'veil of ignorance' about your place.

readings.author: John Rawls
readings.title: A Theory of Justice
readings.note: Principles chosen behind a veil of ignorance.

readings.author: John Rawls
readings.title: Justice as Fairness: A Restatement
readings.note: Rawls's late clarification.

concepts.gloss: Power and knowledge produce each other; discipline shapes the modern subject.

readings.author: Michel Foucault
readings.title: Discipline and Punish
readings.note: How discipline produces the modern subject.

readings.author: Michel Foucault
readings.title: The History of Sexuality, Vol. I
readings.note: Power as productive, not merely repressive.

concepts.gloss: Order comes from strict, impartial law and clear rewards and punishments.

readings.author: Han Feizi
readings.title: Han Feizi
readings.note: Statecraft by strict, impartial law.

readings.author: Shang Yang
readings.title: The Book of Lord Shang
readings.note: The hard logic of reward and punishment.

concepts.gloss: Rulers should lead by moral example; the people follow virtue, not force.

readings.author: Confucius
readings.title: Analects
readings.note: Rule by moral example, not coercion.

readings.author: Mencius
readings.title: Mencius
readings.note: The people's welfare and the Mandate.

concepts.gloss: Impartial concern for all yields the greatest social benefit.

readings.author: Mozi
readings.title: Mozi ('Universal Love')
readings.note: Impartial care as the path to order.

concepts.gloss: Nonviolent resistance — truth-force — as a means of political change.

readings.author: Mahatma Gandhi
readings.title: Hind Swaraj
readings.note: The case for nonviolent self-rule.

readings.author: Mahatma Gandhi
readings.title: An Autobiography
readings.note: Truth-force, tested in a life.

concepts.gloss: Every event is caused; free will is an illusion we cannot escape.

readings.author: Baron d'Holbach
readings.title: The System of Nature
readings.note: Every choice as a link in a causal chain.

readings.author: Sam Harris
readings.title: Free Will
readings.note: The brisk contemporary case against it.

concepts.gloss: We have genuine, undetermined freedom to choose otherwise.

readings.author: Thomas Reid
readings.title: Essays on the Active Powers of Man
readings.note: Agent causation and real freedom.

readings.author: Robert Kane
readings.title: The Significance of Free Will
readings.note: A modern indeterminist defense.

concepts.gloss: Freedom is acting on your own desires — fully compatible with causation.

readings.author: David Hume
readings.title: Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (§8)
readings.note: Liberty reconciled with necessity.

readings.author: Daniel Dennett
readings.title: Elbow Room
readings.note: The varieties of free will worth wanting.

concepts.gloss: Some things are up to us, some are not; love your fate and align your will.

readings.author: Epictetus
readings.title: Enchiridion
readings.note: Will what happens; be free.

readings.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
readings.title: Ecce Homo
readings.note: 'My formula for greatness… amor fati.'

concepts.gloss: Acts bear fruit; you are bound by deeds yet morally responsible for them.

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: Bhagavad Gita
readings.note: Act, but without grasping at the fruit.

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: The Principal Upanishads
readings.note: The earliest doctrine of deeds and rebirth.

concepts.gloss: Minds and bodies don't interact; God synchronized them in advance.

readings.author: G.W. Leibniz
readings.title: Monadology
readings.note: Minds and bodies synchronized by God.

readings.author: G.W. Leibniz
readings.title: Discourse on Metaphysics
readings.note: The harmony first worked out.

concepts.gloss: We are 'condemned to be free' — radically responsible, with no excuses.

readings.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
readings.title: Being and Nothingness
readings.note: No excuses: we are our choices.

readings.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
readings.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
readings.note: Freedom and responsibility, plainly.

concepts.gloss: Live as if you must relive each moment forever; will it, and be free.

readings.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
readings.title: The Gay Science (§341)
readings.note: 'This life… once more and innumerable times.'

readings.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
readings.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
readings.note: The thought dramatized.

concepts.gloss: The sense of being an independent agent is itself part of the illusion (maya).

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: Ashtavakra Gita
readings.note: The witness beyond the sense of agency.

readings.author: Ādi Śaṅkara
readings.title: Vivekachudamani
readings.note: Discriminating the Self from the doer.

concepts.gloss: Life involves suffering; its cause is craving; it can cease; a path leads out.

readings.author: (Pali Canon)
readings.title: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta
readings.note: The Buddha's first discourse.

readings.author: Walpola Rahula
readings.title: What the Buddha Taught
readings.note: The clearest short exposition.

concepts.gloss: The blowing-out of craving; liberation from the cycle of suffering.

readings.author: (Pali Canon)
readings.title: Dhammapada
readings.note: Verses on the extinguishing of craving.

readings.author: Bhikkhu Bodhi
readings.title: In the Buddha's Words
readings.note: Source passages, thematically arranged.

concepts.gloss: A practical path — right view, intention, speech, action, and more — out of suffering.

readings.author: (Pali Canon)
readings.title: Magga-vibhaṅga Sutta
readings.note: The eight factors, defined.

readings.author: Bhikkhu Bodhi
readings.title: The Noble Eightfold Path
readings.note: A practical, faithful walk-through.

concepts.gloss: Suffering loosens its grip when we stop clinging to what changes.

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: Bhagavad Gita
readings.note: Action without clinging to results.

readings.author: (Pali Canon)
readings.title: Dhammapada
readings.note: Letting go as the end of sorrow.

concepts.gloss: Bound to a cycle of rebirth, the soul seeks moksha — release into the absolute.

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: The Principal Upanishads
readings.note: Bondage to rebirth and release from it.

readings.author: (Anonymous)
readings.title: Bhagavad Gita
readings.note: Paths of action, devotion, and knowledge.

concepts.gloss: Peace is freedom from destructive passions; master judgment, accept what is.

readings.author: Epictetus
readings.title: Discourses
readings.note: Mastering judgment to free the mind.

readings.author: Marcus Aurelius
readings.title: Meditations
readings.note: Daily practice of acceptance.

concepts.gloss: Tranquility — an unshakable calm — is the highest human good.

readings.author: Epicurus
readings.title: Letter to Menoeceus
readings.note: Calm as freedom from fear and pain.

readings.author: Sextus Empiricus
readings.title: Outlines of Pyrrhonism
readings.note: Tranquility through suspended judgment.

concepts.gloss: Life is suffering driven by blind Will; release comes from denying that will.

readings.author: Arthur Schopenhauer
readings.title: The World as Will and Representation
readings.note: Suffering from blind Will, and its denial.

readings.author: Arthur Schopenhauer
readings.title: Essays and Aphorisms
readings.note: The accessible late essays.

concepts.gloss: Life has no given meaning; we find peace by revolting and living fully anyway.

readings.author: Albert Camus
readings.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
readings.note: Living without appeal, and revolt.

readings.author: Albert Camus
readings.title: The Rebel
readings.note: From the absurd to rebellion and limits.

concepts.gloss: We endure almost any 'how' if we have a 'why'; meaning is the antidote to despair.

readings.author: Viktor Frankl
readings.title: Man's Search for Meaning
readings.note: Meaning as the will that survives suffering.

concepts.gloss: Deepest well-being arrives in absorbed, skillful action for its own sake.

readings.author: Mihály Csíkszentmihályi
readings.title: Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience
readings.note: Absorption as the shape of well-being.

concepts.gloss: The chain of motion needs a first cause that moves all without itself moving.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Metaphysics (Book XII)
readings.note: The first cause that moves without moving.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Physics (Book VIII)
readings.note: Why motion needs a first source.

concepts.gloss: All reality emanates from a single ineffable source, 'the One,' and longs to return.

readings.author: Plotinus
readings.title: The Enneads
readings.note: All things emanate from, and yearn for, the One.

concepts.gloss: God is that than which none greater can be conceived — so God must exist.

readings.author: Anselm of Canterbury
readings.title: Proslogion
readings.note: That than which none greater can be conceived.

readings.author: Alvin Plantinga
readings.title: The Nature of Necessity
readings.note: The modern modal reformulation.

concepts.gloss: Five rational paths — from motion, cause, contingency — argue to God.

readings.author: Thomas Aquinas
readings.title: Summa Theologiae (I, Q.2)
readings.note: Five rational paths to God.

concepts.gloss: God is not beyond the world; God is the world, 'Deus sive Natura.'

readings.author: Baruch Spinoza
readings.title: Ethics
readings.note: 'Deus sive Natura' — God, or Nature.

concepts.gloss: Reason can't settle God's existence, so weigh the bet — belief is the safer wager.

readings.author: Blaise Pascal
readings.title: Pensées
readings.note: Belief framed as a rational bet.

concepts.gloss: The absolute is beyond words; we can say only what God is not.

readings.author: Pseudo-Dionysius
readings.title: The Mystical Theology
readings.note: Knowing God by unsaying.

readings.author: Maimonides
readings.title: The Guide for the Perplexed
readings.note: Negative attributes of the divine.

concepts.gloss: 'God is dead' — the absolute foundation of values has collapsed; now what?

readings.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
readings.title: The Gay Science (§125)
readings.note: The madman's announcement.

readings.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
readings.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
readings.note: Living after the old values fall.

concepts.gloss: Ultimate reality (emptiness) is no creator-God but the absence of fixed ground.

readings.author: Nāgārjuna
readings.title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā
readings.note: An ultimate that is no creator-ground.

readings.author: Jay Garfield (trans.)
readings.title: The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way
readings.note: Translation with commentary.

concepts.gloss: Consciousness is not a chain of links but a continuous, flowing river.

readings.author: William James
readings.title: The Principles of Psychology
readings.note: The chapter that named the flowing mind.

readings.author: William James
readings.title: The Varieties of Religious Experience
readings.note: Consciousness at its edges.

concepts.gloss: Why is there any felt experience at all? Why isn't it all dark inside?

readings.author: David Chalmers
readings.title: Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness
readings.note: The paper that coined the 'hard problem.'

readings.author: David Chalmers
readings.title: The Conscious Mind
readings.note: The full argument for irreducible experience.

concepts.gloss: A state is conscious if there is something it is like to be in it.

readings.author: Thomas Nagel
readings.title: What Is It Like to Be a Bat?
readings.note: The classic case for subjectivity.

concepts.gloss: Mind goes all the way down; experience is a basic feature of matter.

readings.author: Galen Strawson
readings.title: Realistic Monism: Why Physicalism Entails Panpsychism
readings.note: The sharpest modern argument.

readings.author: Alfred North Whitehead
readings.title: Process and Reality
readings.note: Experience built into the fabric of events.

concepts.gloss: Tononi's theory: consciousness is integrated information — the more a system unifies, the more, it proposes, it feels.

readings.author: Giulio Tononi
readings.title: Phi: A Voyage from the Brain to the Soul
readings.note: IIT for the general reader.

readings.author: Christof Koch
readings.title: The Feeling of Life Itself
readings.note: A neuroscientist's case for IIT.

concepts.gloss: Predict a mind by treating it as a rational agent with beliefs and desires.

readings.author: Daniel Dennett
readings.title: The Intentional Stance
readings.note: Minds as patterns we predict by.

readings.author: Daniel Dennett
readings.title: Consciousness Explained
readings.note: The 'multiple drafts' model of mind.

concepts.gloss: The 'I' is a self-referential loop the brain spins up to model itself.

readings.author: Douglas Hofstadter
readings.title: Gödel, Escher, Bach
readings.note: Self-reference as the root of mind.

readings.author: Douglas Hofstadter
readings.title: I Am a Strange Loop
readings.note: The self as a loop, stated directly.

concepts.gloss: Cognition is enacted by a living body coping with its world, not computed in the skull.

readings.author: Varela, Thompson & Rosch
readings.title: The Embodied Mind
readings.note: Cognitive science meets Buddhist practice.

readings.author: Evan Thompson
readings.title: Mind in Life
readings.note: The mature statement of enactivism.

concepts.gloss: We are born with a deep grammar; language unfolds, it isn't merely learned.

readings.author: Noam Chomsky
readings.title: Aspects of the Theory of Syntax
readings.note: The innate language faculty.

readings.author: Noam Chomsky
readings.title: A Review of B. F. Skinner's Verbal Behavior
readings.note: The blow that ended behaviorism's reign.

concepts.gloss: Two systems think for us — one fast and intuitive, one slow and deliberate.

readings.author: Daniel Kahneman
readings.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
readings.note: System 1 and System 2, for everyone.

concepts.gloss: Mind and matter are two arrangements of one neutral, more basic stuff.

readings.author: Bertrand Russell
readings.title: The Analysis of Mind
readings.note: Mind and matter from one neutral stuff.

readings.author: William James
readings.title: Does 'Consciousness' Exist?
readings.note: The essay that seeded the view.

concepts.gloss: The child's mind builds reality through distinct stages of growth.

readings.author: Jean Piaget
readings.title: The Origins of Intelligence in Children
readings.note: How the infant mind constructs the world.

concepts.gloss: Minds and machines alike steer by loops of feedback and correction.

readings.author: Norbert Wiener
readings.title: Cybernetics
readings.note: Control and communication in animal and machine.

readings.author: Gregory Bateson
readings.title: Steps to an Ecology of Mind
readings.note: Feedback applied to mind and society.

concepts.gloss: Am I a man who dreamt he was a butterfly, or a butterfly dreaming he is a man?

readings.author: Zhuangzi
readings.title: Zhuangzi (Inner Chapters)
readings.note: The butterfly dream and the fluid self.

readings.author: Burton Watson (trans.)
readings.title: The Complete Works of Zhuangzi
readings.note: The standard, readable English Zhuangzi.

concepts.gloss: No meaning is handed down; we forge it through our choices and projects.

readings.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
readings.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
readings.note: We are the authors of our values.

readings.author: Albert Camus
readings.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
readings.note: Meaning made in the teeth of the absurd.

concepts.gloss: Each thing has a telos, an end it is for; the good life fulfils ours.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Nicomachean Ethics
readings.note: The human telos and the good.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Physics (Book II)
readings.note: Final causes — the 'that for the sake of which.'

concepts.gloss: A 'reason for being' — the daily sense that life is worth waking for.

readings.author: Mieko Kamiya
readings.title: Ikigai ni tsuite (On the Meaning of Life)
readings.note: The foundational Japanese study.

readings.author: Ken Mogi
readings.title: The Little Book of Ikigai
readings.note: A short modern introduction.

concepts.gloss: Study not only illness but flourishing — engagement, relationship, meaning.

readings.author: Martin Seligman
readings.title: Authentic Happiness
readings.note: The founding popular statement.

readings.author: Martin Seligman
readings.title: Flourish
readings.note: The PERMA model of well-being.

concepts.gloss: 'The unexamined life is not worth living' — meaning through self-scrutiny.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Apology
readings.note: Socrates on trial: the examined life.

concepts.gloss: Change the thought and you change the feeling; rework distorted beliefs.

readings.author: Aaron Beck
readings.title: Cognitive Therapy and the Emotional Disorders
readings.note: The clinical foundation of CBT.

readings.author: Albert Ellis
readings.title: Reason and Emotion in Psychotherapy
readings.note: REBT — the Stoic root made therapy.

concepts.gloss: Non-judgmental present-moment awareness, carried from Buddhism into medicine.

readings.author: Jon Kabat-Zinn
readings.title: Full Catastrophe Living
readings.note: The MBSR program in full.

readings.author: Jon Kabat-Zinn
readings.title: Wherever You Go, There You Are
readings.note: The gentle way in.

concepts.gloss: Stop struggling with inner experience; accept it and act on your values.

readings.author: Steven C. Hayes
readings.title: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy
readings.note: Psychological flexibility, defined.

readings.author: Steven C. Hayes
readings.title: Get Out of Your Mind and Into Your Life
readings.note: ACT for the general reader.

concepts.gloss: Becoming whole by integrating the shadow and the unconscious into the self.

readings.author: Carl Jung
readings.title: Two Essays on Analytical Psychology
readings.note: The path toward becoming whole.

readings.author: Carl Jung
readings.title: Memories, Dreams, Reflections
readings.note: Individuation lived, in his own voice.

concepts.gloss: Yoga is the stilling of the mind's fluctuations, freeing pure awareness.

readings.author: Patañjali
readings.title: Yoga Sūtras
readings.note: 'Yoga is the stilling of the mind.'

readings.author: Edwin Bryant (trans.)
readings.title: The Yoga Sutras of Patañjali
readings.note: Translation with deep commentary.

concepts.gloss: Enlightenment is not earned by degrees but breaks open all at once.

readings.author: Huineng
readings.title: The Platform Sutra of the Sixth Patriarch
readings.note: The charter of sudden-awakening Chan.

readings.author: (comp.) Linji
readings.title: The Record of Linji
readings.note: Zen's most ferocious teacher.

concepts.gloss: The mind already knows the good; growth is polishing that knowing into action.

readings.author: Wang Yangming
readings.title: Instructions for Practical Living
readings.note: Innate knowing and the unity of knowledge and action.

concepts.gloss: Map human growth across stages, states and perspectives all at once.

readings.author: Ken Wilber
readings.title: Sex, Ecology, Spirituality
readings.note: The four-quadrant integral model.

readings.author: Ken Wilber
readings.title: A Brief History of Everything
readings.note: The accessible overview.

concepts.gloss: The self dissolves; lover and Beloved become one.

readings.author: Jalal al-Din Rumi
readings.title: The Masnavi
readings.note: The great poem of longing and union.

readings.author: Ibn Arabi
readings.title: The Bezels of Wisdom
readings.note: The metaphysics of divine self-disclosure.

concepts.gloss: All that exists is one divine reality; creation is God's self-disclosure.

readings.author: Ibn Arabi
readings.title: The Meccan Revelations
readings.note: The vast summa of waḥdat al-wujūd.

readings.author: William Chittick
readings.title: The Sufi Path of Knowledge
readings.note: The standard scholarly guide to Ibn Arabi.

concepts.gloss: Floating, senseless, you would still know you exist — the self is no mere body.

readings.author: Avicenna
readings.title: The Book of Healing (Kitāb al-Shifāʾ)
readings.note: The 'floating man' proves the self to itself.

concepts.gloss: Reason cannot reach the deepest truths; even cause and effect rest on God's will.

readings.author: Al-Ghazali
readings.title: The Incoherence of the Philosophers
readings.note: The great critique of philosophy's reach.

readings.author: Al-Ghazali
readings.title: Deliverance from Error
readings.note: His spiritual and intellectual autobiography.

concepts.gloss: Words mean by their use in forms of life, not by mirroring the world.

readings.author: Ludwig Wittgenstein
readings.title: Philosophical Investigations
readings.note: Meaning as use; the later Wittgenstein.

readings.author: Ludwig Wittgenstein
readings.title: Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus
readings.note: The picture theory he later overturned.

concepts.gloss: True ideas are the ones that work — that pay their way in experience.

readings.author: William James
readings.title: Pragmatism
readings.note: Truth as what works, in lectures.

readings.author: Charles S. Peirce
readings.title: How to Make Our Ideas Clear
readings.note: The pragmatic maxim at the source.

concepts.gloss: Design without a designer: life's complexity from variation and selection.

readings.author: Charles Darwin
readings.title: On the Origin of Species
readings.note: Design without a designer.

readings.author: Daniel Dennett
readings.title: Darwin's Dangerous Idea
readings.note: Evolution as 'universal acid' for philosophy.

concepts.gloss: Politics runs on power, not piety; the prince must master fortune and force.

readings.author: Niccolò Machiavelli
readings.title: The Prince
readings.note: Power as it is, not as it ought to be.

readings.author: Niccolò Machiavelli
readings.title: Discourses on Livy
readings.note: The republican counterpart to The Prince.

concepts.gloss: World and souls are real parts of God — one, yet not featureless.

readings.author: Ramanuja
readings.title: Sri Bhashya
readings.note: The qualified-non-dual reply to Shankara.

concepts.gloss: Modern life is mediated by images; we watch representations where we once lived.

readings.author: Guy Debord
readings.title: The Society of the Spectacle
readings.note: Life mediated by images, in 221 theses.

concepts.gloss: No longer disciplined from outside, we exploit ourselves — and call it freedom.

readings.author: Byung-Chul Han
readings.title: The Burnout Society
readings.note: The achievement-subject who exploits itself.

readings.author: Byung-Chul Han
readings.title: The Transparency Society
readings.note: The companion critique of compulsory openness.

concepts.gloss: Everyday life is theatre; the self is a performance staged for an audience.

readings.author: Erving Goffman
readings.title: The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life
readings.note: Social life as dramaturgy, front-stage and back. (Edinburgh monograph; US ed. 1959.)

concepts.gloss: Copies without originals; the map has replaced the territory.

readings.author: Jean Baudrillard
readings.title: Simulacra and Simulation
readings.note: Signs that refer only to other signs.

concepts.gloss: Consumer society manufactures false needs and quietly absorbs all dissent.

readings.author: Herbert Marcuse
readings.title: One-Dimensional Man
readings.note: How affluence pacifies and flattens dissent.

concepts.gloss: It is easier to imagine the end of the world than the end of capitalism.

readings.author: Mark Fisher
readings.title: Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?
readings.note: Why the future feels cancelled.

concepts.gloss: Whole careers feel pointless — work as moral and social performance.

readings.author: David Graeber
readings.title: Bullshit Jobs: A Theory
readings.note: On the spread of pointless work.

readings.author: David Graeber
readings.title: Debt: The First 5,000 Years
readings.note: The deeper anthropology of obligation.

concepts.gloss: Labour, work and action — and the modern eclipse of public life by mere making.

readings.author: Hannah Arendt
readings.title: The Human Condition
readings.note: Labour, work and action — and the lost public realm.

concepts.gloss: Strip wants to the bone, owe society nothing, be free as a dog in the sun.

readings.author: Diogenes Laertius
readings.title: Lives of Eminent Philosophers (Book VI)
readings.note: The surviving source for Diogenes' life.

readings.author: R. Bracht Branham & M.-O. Goulet-Cazé (eds.)
readings.title: The Cynics
readings.note: The standard modern overview.

concepts.gloss: 'Lathe biōsas' — live unnoticed; step out of the race for status.

readings.author: Epicurus
readings.title: Principal Doctrines & Vatican Sayings
readings.note: Where 'live unnoticed' takes root.

readings.author: Epicurus
readings.title: Letter to Menoeceus
readings.note: The ethics of withdrawal and calm.

concepts.gloss: Trust the self over the crowd; withdraw to nature to hear your own voice.

readings.author: Ralph Waldo Emerson
readings.title: Self-Reliance
readings.note: Trust thyself; the great essay of nonconformity.

readings.author: Henry David Thoreau
readings.title: Walden
readings.note: Deliberate withdrawal as an experiment in living.

concepts.gloss: Zuowang — 'sit and forget' body and mind until you merge with the Way.

readings.author: Zhuangzi
readings.title: Zhuangzi ('The Great and Venerable Teacher')
readings.note: The dialogue on zuowang, sitting in forgetting.

readings.author: Burton Watson (trans.)
readings.title: The Complete Works of Zhuangzi
readings.note: The readable standard English text.

concepts.gloss: Two modes of living: grasping at having, or simply being.

readings.author: Erich Fromm
readings.title: To Have or to Be?
readings.note: Two modes of existence, having versus being.

readings.author: Erich Fromm
readings.title: The Art of Loving
readings.note: The accessible gateway to Fromm.

concepts.gloss: Facing one's own death calls a life back from the crowd into authenticity.

readings.author: Martin Heidegger
readings.title: Being and Time (Division II)
readings.note: Authentic existence as being-toward-death.

concepts.gloss: Civilization is a vast defense mechanism against the terror of mortality.

readings.author: Ernest Becker
readings.title: The Denial of Death
readings.note: Heroism and culture as defenses against mortality.

concepts.gloss: Death as a passage to be met with awareness — the bardo between lives.

readings.author: Karma Lingpa (rev.)
readings.title: The Tibetan Book of the Dead (Bardo Thödol)
readings.note: Revealed as terma; traditionally attributed to the 8th-c. Padmasambhava.

readings.author: Robert Thurman (trans.)
readings.title: The Tibetan Book of the Dead
readings.note: A clear, complete modern rendering.

concepts.gloss: Running a program is not understanding; syntax never adds up to meaning.

readings.author: John Searle
readings.title: Minds, Brains, and Programs
readings.note: The thought experiment against strong AI.

concepts.gloss: Metzinger argues no one ever had a self — only a transparent model the brain mistakes for one.

readings.author: Thomas Metzinger
readings.title: The Ego Tunnel
readings.note: No-self neuroscience for the general reader.

readings.author: Thomas Metzinger
readings.title: Being No One
readings.note: The dense self-model theory beneath it.

concepts.gloss: Perception is the brain's best guess — a hallucination reined in by the senses.

readings.author: Anil Seth
readings.title: Being You: A New Science of Consciousness
readings.note: Perception as a brain-built best guess.

readings.author: Andy Clark
readings.title: Surfing Uncertainty
readings.note: The predictive-processing framework in depth.

concepts.gloss: Consciousness is information broadcast to the whole brain from a central stage.

readings.author: Stanislas Dehaene
readings.title: Consciousness and the Brain
readings.note: The global-workspace evidence, accessibly told.

readings.author: Bernard Baars
readings.title: A Cognitive Theory of Consciousness
readings.note: The original theatre-of-mind model.

concepts.gloss: What matters is psychological continuity, not a deep further fact of 'me'.

readings.author: Derek Parfit
readings.title: Reasons and Persons
readings.note: Personal identity dissolved by argument.

concepts.gloss: On Kastrup's idealism, reality is fundamentally mental; matter is how mind looks from outside.

readings.author: Bernardo Kastrup
readings.title: The Idea of the World
readings.note: The rigorous case for a mental universe.

readings.author: Bernardo Kastrup
readings.title: Why Materialism Is Baloney
readings.note: The polemical, accessible version.

concepts.gloss: Hoffman's interface theory: evolution tuned perception for fitness, not truth, so spacetime may be just our interface.

readings.author: Donald Hoffman
readings.title: The Case Against Reality
readings.note: Perception as a fitness-tuned user interface.

concepts.gloss: The hemispheres offer two ways of attending to the world — and ours is unbalanced.

readings.author: Iain McGilchrist
readings.title: The Master and His Emissary
readings.note: The two hemispheres as two worlds.

concepts.gloss: Money, nations and rights are stories — fictions that let strangers cooperate.

readings.author: Yuval Noah Harari
readings.title: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
readings.note: Cooperation through shared imagined orders.

concepts.gloss: Gender is not what you are but what you repeatedly do — an act with no actor behind it.

readings.author: Judith Butler
readings.title: Gender Trouble
readings.note: Gender as repeated performative acts.

concepts.gloss: Freedom is observing without the observer — no method, no path, no guru.

readings.author: Jiddu Krishnamurti
readings.title: Freedom from the Known
readings.note: Seeing without the screen of the past.

readings.author: Jiddu Krishnamurti
readings.title: The First and Last Freedom
readings.note: The early, wide-ranging talks.

concepts.gloss: The brain stirs before we feel we 'decided' — though whether that dooms free will is disputed, as Schurger reads it as mere noise accumulating.

readings.author: Benjamin Libet
readings.title: Mind Time
readings.note: The famous timing experiments and their meaning.

concepts.gloss: Screen-life thins out embodied, face-to-face experience and the self it grew.

readings.author: Christine Rosen
readings.title: The Extinction of Experience
readings.note: How a mediated life erodes the embodied one.

concepts.gloss: Ethics begins in the face of the Other — an infinite claim that comes before the self.

readings.author: Emmanuel Levinas
readings.title: Totality and Infinity
readings.note: Ethics as first philosophy; the Other's infinite claim.

readings.author: Emmanuel Levinas
readings.title: Otherwise than Being
readings.note: The later, denser account of responsibility.

concepts.gloss: Two ways of meeting the world: the detached I–It, or the whole-hearted I–Thou.

readings.author: Martin Buber
readings.title: I and Thou
readings.note: The classic of dialogical, relational existence.

concepts.gloss: Goodness as the patient, loving attention that unselfs us before the real.

readings.author: Iris Murdoch
readings.title: The Sovereignty of Good
readings.note: Attention and 'unselfing' as the moral work.

readings.author: Simone Weil
readings.title: Gravity and Grace
readings.note: Attention, affliction and grace, in aphorisms.

readings.author: Simone Weil
readings.title: Waiting for God
readings.note: The accessible gateway to Weil.

concepts.gloss: Justice measured by what people are actually able to do and to be.

readings.author: Martha Nussbaum
readings.title: Women and Human Development
readings.note: The capabilities list as a theory of justice.

readings.author: Amartya Sen
readings.title: Development as Freedom
readings.note: Freedom as the real measure of development.

concepts.gloss: The twentieth-century revival of character over rules and consequences.

readings.author: G.E.M. Anscombe
readings.title: Modern Moral Philosophy
readings.note: The essay that reopened virtue and character.

readings.author: Alasdair MacIntyre
readings.title: After Virtue
readings.note: Virtue, tradition and narrative against modern moralism.

readings.author: Philippa Foot
readings.title: Natural Goodness
readings.note: Grounding virtue in a form of life.

concepts.gloss: Compassion turned outward — meditation in the service of the suffering world.

readings.author: Thich Nhat Hanh
readings.title: The Miracle of Mindfulness
readings.note: Mindfulness as practice for living and serving.

readings.author: B.R. Ambedkar
readings.title: The Buddha and His Dhamma
readings.note: Buddhism reframed for social liberation.

arguments.title: Permanence vs. Flux
arguments.note: Does anything stay the same, or is all becoming?

arguments.title: Reason vs. Experience
arguments.note: Where does real knowledge come from?

arguments.title: Mind & Body
arguments.note: What is the mind, and how does it relate to the body?

arguments.title: Is There a Self?
arguments.note: A famous convergence of East and West.

arguments.title: Are We Free?
arguments.note: The oldest standoff in ethics — now with a lab.

arguments.title: After God
arguments.note: What happens to meaning when the Absolute withdraws?

arguments.title: What Is Consciousness?
arguments.note: The defining puzzle of modern mind science.

arguments.title: How Do We Change?
arguments.note: Three routes to transforming a mind.

arguments.title: Innate or Learned?
arguments.note: Where does the mind's structure come from?

arguments.title: Is the Self a Performance?
arguments.note: From staging, to self-exploitation, to a way out.

arguments.title: Turn Away, or Turn Toward?
arguments.note: The book's own fault line: refusal versus relation.
